title: Quantum and classical noise characteristics of parametrically driven cavity solitons in dispersive Kerr resonators
abstract: Temporal cavity solitons generated in monochromatically driven dispersive Kerr resonators offer an attractive avenue for on-chip optical frequency comb generation. Key to many of their applications is to understand how noise -- both technical and quantum -- affects the soliton states, which has accordingly been extensively investigated. Here, we report on a comprehensive theoretical study that elucidates how technical and quantum fluctuations impact a new type of soliton structure that has very recently been predicted and observed in dispersive Kerr resonators under conditions of bichromatic driving: the pure-Kerr parametrically driven cavity soliton (PDCS). We examine how classical laser phase noise transfers from the two pump fields onto the soliton frequency comb, and we calculate the solitons' fundamental quantum-limited timing jitter and two-mode squeezing spectra. In each case, we find that PDCSs can out-perform conventional cavity solitons with comparable characteristics, even when driven by two uncorrelated lasers. Our results demonstrate that pure-Kerr PDCSs can offer unprecedented performance in noise-sensitive photonic applications and as a quantum resource
summary: This theoretical study investigates the noise properties of parametrically driven cavity solitons (PDCSs) in Kerr resonators compared to conventional solitons. By analyzing quantum and classical noise sources, the authors demonstrate that PDCSs offer superior performance, particularly in terms of reduced timing jitter and enhanced quantum squeezing. This suggests PDCSs are highly promising for next-generation, low-noise photonic quantum applications.
main_problem: The study aims to elucidate how both technical (classical) and quantum noise sources affect the unique pure-Kerr parametrically driven cavity soliton (PDCS) structure.
main_result: PDCSs are shown to outperform conventional cavity solitons across key metrics, exhibiting lower fundamental timing jitter, reduced phase noise, and stronger two-mode squeezing.
method: The authors employ theoretical modeling, analyzing the system using the generalized Lugiato-Lefever equation and linearizing quantum fluctuations around the mean field.
model_or_system: The physical system is a dispersive Kerr resonator driven by bichromatic fields, modeled by the Lugiato-Lefever equation. The analysis quantizes this system by treating fluctuations as operators and solving the resulting linear equations in the frequency domain.
key_observables: Fundamental quantum-limited timing jitter, two-mode squeezing spectra, and phase noise transfer from driving lasers.
important_parameters: The relative strength of noise ($\epsilon$), the mode number ($l$), and the detuning/parametric driving strength ($\Delta_{	ext{eff}}, \mu$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes that the system can be linearized around the mean field, and for noise calculations, the noise is assumed to be white and Gaussian.
figures_summary: Figures compare line profiles and normalized linewidths for CS vs. PDCS, and show the two-mode quadrature spectra, demonstrating superior squeezing and narrower lines for PDCSs.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops the model from the classical LLE to the quantized fluctuation equations, deriving analytical and numerical methods to calculate noise spectra and jitter, culminating in a direct comparison between PDCS and CS performance.
why_interesting: This work bridges quantum optics and nonlinear wave physics by quantifying noise resilience in a novel optical soliton system, providing concrete metrics (squeezing, jitter) for quantum resource assessment.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) and (b) show schematic illustrations of (a) CS and (b) PDCS generation in a monochromatically and bichromat- ically driven dispersive χ(3) Kerr resonator, respectively. (c) and (d) show numerically simulated temporal and spectral profiles, respectively, of a PDCS (solid blue curve) and a CS (red dashed curve). To isolate the soliton component, ES(τ), the constant background was removed from the CS field and a spectral band-pass filter (shaded green region in (d) shows the pass-band; inset in (c) shows the intensity profiles before background removal) was used on the PDCS before obtaining the intensity profiles in (c). PDCS simulations use d2 = −2 and d4 = 0.02, ∆± = 0, S± ≈1.89,...

FIG. 2. (a) Numerically simulated line profile of the l = 0 comb mode for a PDCS (solid blue curve) and a correspond- ing CS (red dashed curve). Black circles show the pump pro- file used in the simulations. (b) 3-dB widths of the PDCS (solid blue curve) and CS (red dashed curve) comb lines as a function of relative mode number l. Vertical black lines indi- cate the pumped modes in PDCS simulations, and horizontal dash-dotted magenta line indicates the pump linewidth di- vided by two. All simulation parameters as in Fig. 1. The results were obtained by averaging over 500 independent noise realizations.

FIG. 4. (a) Two-mode quadrature spectra for mode pair l = 1 for a PDCS (solid blue curve) and corresponding CS (dashed red curve). Both spectra are calculated for their respective optimal mixing angle ϕmin: for the PDCS case, ϕmin = 1.26; for the CS case, ϕmin = 2.18. (b) The minimal value attained by the two-mode quadrature spectra at the optimal mixing angle ϕmin for a PDCS (blue circles) and a corresponding CS (red diamonds), as a function of mode pair number. Solid and dashed curves are guides to the eye. Parameters as in Fig. 1 with critical coupling assumed (squeezing depends on the coupling conditions – see Supplementary Information).
